mappings.rationaleAI: Covers the full scope of digital and analog computers (D.1), programming systems and software (D.2), computer languages (D.3), microcomputer and supercomputer applications (D.4), and artificial intelligence developments (D.5); also traces calculating devices from abacus through mechanical calculators directly relevant to B.
Directly covers telephone and telecommunications systems and equipment (I.4), tracing the technology from Bell's first device through PSTN, cordless, cellular, and VoIP systems.

Spans the major communications systems of the outline — telegraph (I.3), telephone (I.4), radio (I.5), and the internet (I.8) — and covers modulation, bandwidth, multiplexing, and network architecture fundamental to electronic components and techniques (K).
Covers radio communications systems (I.5) and television communications systems (I.6), the two principal broadcast media addressed in the outline's major communications section.

Directly addresses electronic components (K.1) including active components such as transistors and diodes (K.1.a), passive components (K.1.b), and integrated circuits/microprocessors (K.1.c) used in communications technology.
Covers still photography technology (G.1) and motion-picture and television photography (G.2), directly corresponding to the outline's technology of photography subsections.

Directly corresponds to outline path D.5, covering developments in AI including expert systems, natural-language processing, computer vision, and the neural network and deep learning methods that underpin these applications.

Mobile phones are a primary form of telephone and telecommunications equipment (I.4) and smartphones constitute key nodes in electronic networks (I.8), encompassing cellular radio systems, data networking, and convergent communications.

Turing's formalization of algorithm and computation is foundational to computer programming systems (D.2), and his Turing test and early stored-program computer design are central to the history of artificial intelligence (D.5) and computers broadly (D).

Shannon's information theory — quantifying information in bits — is the direct subject of outline path A (communication and information theory), and the article's treatment of encoding, storage, transmission, and compression corresponds to the elements of information processing (H.1).
Covers organization and retrieval including computerized library catalogs and library networks (H.1.c), and library reference services correspond to educational and reference systems (H.2.c).

Covers printing machines and processes (E.3), production of printing plates including woodcut, engraving, etching, and lithography (E.4, E.4.a), and book/newspaper publishing (I.1) as a major communications system.

Directly addresses television communications systems and equipment (I.6), including broadcast engineering, cable, satellite, and digital transmission; also relates to video recording systems (F.2) and motion-picture/television photography (G.2).
Directly covers the history and technology of encryption and decryption, signal security, and message authentication that constitute outline path I.9 on cryptology.
Radar is the primary technology addressed under major systems of detection and remote sensing (J.1), covering pulsed and continuous radio wave systems for ranging, tracking, and velocity measurement.

Covers the technology of photography broadly (G), including still photography (G.1), motion-picture and television photography (G.2), and the progression from chemical film to digital sensors directly relevant to the outline's photographic technology section.
Covers newspaper and magazine publishing (I.1) as a major communication system, and traces news dissemination through postal systems (I.2), broadcasting, and digital media.
Directly addresses book, newspaper, and magazine publishing (I.1), the first major communication system in the outline, encompassing print and digital forms from trade books to e-books and websites.

Books are the primary subject of publishing (I.1) and connect to printing technology (E.3); encyclopaedias as a reference subtype are explicitly noted in H.2.c.ii, making this article relevant to reference information systems as well.

Directly covers electronic networks (I.8) — TCP/IP, the Web, email, and VoIP — and relates to display and dissemination systems including electronic mail and online services (H.1.d) and public online database systems (H.2.b).

Integrated circuits are explicitly identified in outline path K.1.c as miniature arrays of interconnected circuit elements including microprocessors; they are also foundational to all modern computers (D).

Covers holography and laser photography (G.3) and optical disc systems (F.3); laser technology also underpins fiber-optic communications relevant to the telecommunications infrastructure underlying I.

Edison's phonograph is the founding mechanical sound recording system (F.1); his carbon telephone microphone is relevant to I.4; his motion-picture technology relates to G.2; and his printing telegraph work connects to writing and reproducing machines (C.1).

Gutenberg's movable-type press is the historical origin of European printing machines and processes (E.3) and printing plate production (E.4), directly enabling book and newspaper publishing as a communication system (I.1).

Robotics is explicitly listed in outline path D.5 as a development in artificial intelligence, alongside expert systems and natural-language processing, reflecting the close integration of robotics with computer science.

Directly addresses radio communications systems and equipment (I.5) and covers radar as a remote-sensing application of radio waves (J); antennas and waveguides as passive components (K.1.b) are also discussed in the article's technical content.

Covers the process, models, and channels of information transfer that constitute communication and information theory (A), and traces the historical evolution through writing, print, broadcast, and digital technologies that map to the major systems of communication (I).

Characterizes the era defined by computers (D), information technology and data systems (H), and electronic networks including the internet (I.8), providing historical and economic context for the entire section's subject matter.

Video games are a significant application of microcomputers and consumer electronics (D.4), and their audiovisual output systems connect to sound and video reproducing devices (F); the article provides context for interactive digital media as an information processing application.
